metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify (by reading S1's spec or the actual generator changes it introduces) that `gen_mcp_reference.py` and `gen_env_registry.py` will genuinely iterate all of `MCP_ENV_VARS` after S1 — currently both scripts use different mechanisms (a hardcoded 3-var list and AST source scanning respectively) and will NOT automatically pick up new `auth_introspection_*` entries; if S1 does not fix this, S4 must include the generator changes itself.
findings.finding: The plan asserts 'the regenerated `docs/mcp-reference.md`/`docs/env-vars.md` pick these up via S1's fixes making `gen_mcp_reference.py`/`gen_env_registry.py` iterate `MCP_ENV_VARS`.' Reading both scripts refutes this: `gen_mcp_reference.py` renders exactly 3 hardcoded gate-var names from `MCP_ENV_VARS` (line 208: `for var in ('REBAR_MCP_READONLY', 'REBAR_MCP_ALLOW_LLM', 'REBAR_MCP_ALLOW_JIRA_SYNC')`), not a full iteration; `gen_env_registry.py` discovers vars via AST source scanning of `os.environ` literal reads, not via `MCP_ENV_VARS`. If this is what S1 is supposed to fix, S4 depends on S1 delivering changes that are not yet present — and if those changes are not delivered, registering auth vars in `MCP_ENV_VARS` will not cause them to appear in either generated doc.
findings.location: What section ('the regenerated `docs/mcp-reference.md`/`docs/env-vars.md` pick these up via S1's fixes...') and AC line 1
findings.suggested_fix: Add an explicit AC sub-bullet that verifies S1's generator changes are in place before S4 lands, or move the generator-fix work into S4's scope with a concrete description of what each script change must do (e.g., gen_env_registry.py must union in MCP_ENV_VARS entries; gen_mcp_reference.py must iterate all MCP_ENV_VARS in its env-var table rather than the hardcoded 3-var list).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `src/rebar/_config_schema.py` (specifically `McpConfig` and its coercion table entries) to the enumerated edit targets, covering the new `auth_introspection_*` TOML config fields.
findings.finding: `src/rebar/_config_schema.py` (`McpConfig` dataclass and its coercion tables `_SECTION_CLASSES`/`_SECTIONS`) is an unenumerated edit target: the plan's `auth_introspection_*` flat config keys (which the plan says live under `mcp.auth_*` TOML shape) require new fields on `McpConfig` and new coercion entries — but the plan names only `_mcp_auth.py`, `MCP_ENV_VARS`/`server.json`, and the doc generators as edit targets.
findings.location: What section ('flat config keys THIS story registers') / Scope
findings.suggested_fix: Add `auth_introspection_endpoint: str = ''`, `auth_introspection_client_id: str = ''`, etc. as fields on `McpConfig`, and register their coercers in `_SECTIONS['mcp']`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify which `auth_introspection_*` items are env vars (to be added to `MCP_ENV_VARS`/`server.json`) versus TOML config keys (to be added to `McpConfig` in `_config_schema.py`), and update the edit-target list accordingly.
findings.finding: The plan conflates TOML config keys (`auth_introspection_endpoint`, etc.) with `MCP_ENV_VARS` entries (which currently are environment variable names like `REBAR_MCP_READONLY`). Registering TOML config keys in `MCP_ENV_VARS` / `server.json` is architecturally inconsistent with how the existing config surface works, and no translation layer (env-var names vs. TOML keys) is specified.
findings.location: What section ('flat config keys THIS story registers … Registers its keys in MCP_ENV_VARS/server.json')
findings.suggested_fix: Only `auth_introspection_client_secret_env` (the env-var name indirection) and possibly the secret's own env var name belong in MCP_ENV_VARS; endpoint/ttl/allow_* are TOML config keys for McpConfig.

findings.finding: The plan asserts that registering the `auth_introspection_*` keys in `MCP_ENV_VARS` and running `gen_mcp_reference.py` will cause those keys to 'appear in the regenerated reference docs', but the generator's gate-variable table is hardcoded to exactly three vars (`REBAR_MCP_READONLY`, `REBAR_MCP_ALLOW_LLM`, `REBAR_MCP_ALLOW_JIRA_SYNC`). New keys added to `MCP_ENV_VARS` are NOT automatically surfaced in the rendered doc — the script would also need to be updated, but the plan does not mention updating `gen_mcp_reference.py`.
findings.location: What section / AC line 1: 'Registers its keys in `MCP_ENV_VARS`/`server.json` + regenerates the reference docs'
findings.suggested_fix: In `gen_mcp_reference.py`, replace the hardcoded 3-name loop in `render()` with an iteration over all entries in `MCP_ENV_VARS` (or a filtered subset), so any new keys added to the canonical list are automatically included in the rendered gate-variables table.

findings.finding: The cache-expiry semantic — that a cached positive is evicted at `min(now + cache_ttl, token_exp)` and a subsequent call re-hits the introspection endpoint — has no observable oracle in AC4. The two oracles listed cover only cache-key format, not post-TTL re-introspection. Without an explicit assertion that the verifier re-contacts the AS after the cache entry expires, the critical security property (caching does not extend token validity beyond configured TTL or token exp) is untestable from the ACs as written.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC4 (cache behavior block)
findings.suggested_fix: Extend AC4 with: 'and that after the cache entry expires (tested via time-mocking for both the cache_ttl-bound case and the token_exp-bound case), a subsequent verification call re-hits the fake endpoint — confirming the eviction bound is min(now + cache_ttl, token_exp) and not either bound alone.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify whether S1 (not just S2) is a hard dependency of S4, and explicitly enumerate `scripts/gen_env_registry.py` as an edit target (or confirm it is covered by S1) so that adding `auth_introspection_*` keys to `MCP_ENV_VARS` actually populates `docs/env-vars.md`.
findings.finding: `gen_env_registry.py` is a pure AST scanner of `src/rebar` source files and does NOT currently iterate `MCP_ENV_VARS`; the plan's claim that S1's fixes make it iterate `MCP_ENV_VARS` implies a significant refactor of that script, but S1 is not listed in the Scope's dependency list and no edit to `gen_env_registry.py` is enumerated in S4's own edit set.
findings.location: What section / AC line 1 ('via S1's fixes making gen_env_registry.py iterate MCP_ENV_VARS')

findings.finding: The What section states the verifier 'maps `scope`/`exp`/`sub`/`client_id` into an `AccessToken`', naming four response fields as deliverables. However, neither the plan nor any AC specifies which `AccessToken` fields receive `sub` and `client_id` (the SDK's `AccessToken` schema is not reproduced or cited). Only `scopes`, `resource`, and `expires_at` are oracle'd in AC-2; `sub` and `client_id` mapping have no verification path, making them untestable named deliverables.
findings.location: What section — 'maps `scope`/`exp`/`sub`/`client_id` into an `AccessToken`'; AC-2 (unit test assertions)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the enforcement point for a missing/empty `auth_introspection_client_secret_env` env var (recommended: raise `ValueError` at construction time if the named env var is not set or is empty), and add it to the construction-time guard acceptance criterion (AC5).
findings.finding: The plan does not specify what happens when the env var named by `auth_introspection_client_secret_env` is absent or empty at runtime — fail at construction, fail at first call, or treat as unauthenticated. This is a security-relevant defaultable gap: silent fallback to no client auth would bypass the authentication requirement.
findings.location: What section — 'auth_introspection_client_secret_env (NAME of an env var holding the client secret)'
findings.suggested_fix: In the Security section or What section, add: 'If the env var named by `auth_introspection_client_secret_env` is absent or empty at construction time, raise `ValueError` (fail-closed, same guard family as http:// and private-host checks).' Then add this case to AC5's observable oracle.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify in the Security section that a missing or empty `auth_introspection_client_secret_env` value MUST raise at construction time (fail-closed), and add a corresponding AC oracle (construction raises when the named env var is absent).
findings.finding: The plan does not specify the failure mode when the env var named by `auth_introspection_client_secret_env` is absent or empty at runtime. An unset env var could result in an unauthenticated introspection call (no Authorization header) that some permissive Authorization Servers will answer — silently accepting every token without client authentication.
findings.location: Security section — 'client_secret_basic (secret from auth_introspection_client_secret_env)'; AC — 'Unit test asserts construction-time guards: the introspection request carries client_secret_basic (secret sourced from auth_introspection_client_secret_env)'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the Security section: 'If the env var named by auth_introspection_client_secret_env is absent or empty at construction, IntrospectionTokenVerifier raises ConfigError — it never starts without client credentials.' Add to the construction-time-guards AC: 'and that constructing the verifier with an unset/empty client secret env var raises at construction.'

findings.finding: The plan specifies that the client secret is sourced from the env var named by `auth_introspection_client_secret_env`, but does not declare what happens when that env var is absent or empty at startup — whether construction fails (fail-closed) or the verifier proceeds without client authentication (fail-open). An unauthenticated introspection call would violate RFC 7662 §2.1 and the plan's own 'authenticated TLS' requirement, and could allow any bearer to pass validation if the AS accepts unauthenticated introspection.
findings.location: Security section — 'client_secret_basic, secret from auth_introspection_client_secret_env'; What section — 'auth_introspection_client_secret_env (NAME of an env var holding the client secret — no plaintext secret in config)'
findings.suggested_fix: In the Security section, add: 'If the env var named by `auth_introspection_client_secret_env` is absent or empty, construction MUST raise `ConfigError` (fail-closed).' Add a unit-test AC bullet for this case.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Move the `auth_introspection_allow_missing_aud=true` risk disclosure INTO S4 itself (either in the registered config key description in MCP_ENV_VARS / server.json or in the env-vars.md entry), rather than deferring it entirely to S7, so the warning is present when the flag is available.
findings.finding: The security risk of `auth_introspection_allow_missing_aud=true` is stated as 'documented risk in S7,' but S4 is explicitly declared independent of S5/S6/S7 and can ship before S7. An operator enabling the flag before S7 ships will have no in-product documentation of the security tradeoff (aud-absent tokens are accepted, scoping enforcement relies entirely on the AS's other mechanisms).
findings.location: What section — 'auth_introspection_allow_missing_aud (bool, default false)'; Security section — '(documented risk in S7)'; Scope section — 'Independent of S3/S5/S6'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a `description` to the `auth_introspection_allow_missing_aud` entry in MCP_ENV_VARS that reads: 'SECURITY: setting this to true accepts tokens with no aud claim, removing audience binding. Only enable if your AS scopes tokens by other means (e.g., introspection endpoint is RS-specific). Default false (fail-closed).' Remove the '(documented risk in S7)' deferral.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State the default HTTP timeout value for the introspection call (e.g. `timeout=5.0` seconds) in the Security section so the executor does not have to invent it.
findings.finding: The plan specifies a 'bounded timeout' for the introspection HTTP call but provides no default value; an executing agent must choose a number with no guidance, making this a defaultable gap with security/correctness implications (too long enables DoS amplification; too short causes false failures).
findings.location: Security section — 'bounded timeout'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a sentence such as: 'The httpx client uses a default timeout of 5 s (connect + read); this value is not currently configurable via a flat key.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify a concrete default timeout value (e.g., 5 s) for the introspection HTTP call in the Security section and add an AC assertion that the httpx client is constructed with that timeout.
findings.finding: The plan asserts 'bounded timeout' on the introspection HTTP call but specifies no concrete value. Without a numeric done-definition, an implementor can choose any value (e.g., 30 s), leaving a hot-path external I/O call with an operationally silent, arbitrarily long block on every token validation.
findings.location: Security section — 'bounded timeout'; AC line 'Unit test asserts construction-time guards'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to Security: 'The httpx client MUST be constructed with a timeout of ≤ 5 s (connect + read combined); this value is not operator-configurable in S4 (a future story may expose it).' Add a corresponding AC: 'Unit test asserts the httpx client used for introspection is constructed with a timeout ≤ 5 s.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add the `auth_introspection_allow_missing_aud` security risk (audience-bypass caveat) to S4's own deliverables — either as an inline warning in the generated `docs/env-vars.md` key description or as an ADR note — rather than deferring documentation entirely to S7.
findings.finding: The security risk of `auth_introspection_allow_missing_aud=true` is attributed to 'documented risk in S7,' but S4 is described as independent of S5/S6 and presumably shippable before S7. If S4 lands without S7, operators enabling this flag have no in-product or doc-site documentation of the revocation/audience-bypass risk.
findings.location: Security section — 'Audience' bullet; AC bullet 3 ('auth_introspection_allow_missing_aud'); Compatibility section
findings.suggested_fix: In the MCP_ENV_VARS entry for `auth_introspection_allow_missing_aud`, include a `description` or `warning` field that states: 'Setting this to true accepts tokens with no audience claim; only enable if your AS scopes tokens to this resource by other means. Enabling this weakens audience validation (see security tradeoffs in docs/adr/S7).' This makes S4 self-documenting regardless of S7's landing order.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a maximum cache size (e.g., 1 000 entries, LRU eviction) to the cache specification in the Security section and a corresponding AC that asserts entries beyond the cap are evicted.
findings.finding: The in-memory positive cache has no specified size bound. If caching is enabled (cache_ttl > 0) and a service sees many distinct tokens (e.g., short-lived tokens from many users), the cache grows without limit, creating an unbounded memory footprint.
findings.location: Security section — 'Cache: keyed on a hash of the token'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to Security: 'The cache is bounded to a maximum of N entries (default 1 000, not operator-configurable in S4); when full, the oldest entry (LRU) is evicted.' Update the relevant AC to assert that inserting N+1 distinct tokens evicts the oldest.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the concurrency guard for the cache (e.g., an `asyncio.Lock` per token-hash or a single verifier-level lock) so the check-then-act miss-path is atomic and redundant introspection calls cannot race.
findings.finding: The plan does not specify concurrency safety for the in-process cache: under concurrent async (or threaded) requests carrying the same token, multiple coroutines/threads may simultaneously find a cache miss and fire redundant introspection HTTP calls (classic check-then-act TOCTOU). No lock, asyncio.Lock, or CAS mechanism is mentioned.
findings.location: Security section — Cache paragraph; AC bullet 3 (cache observable oracle)
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the Security/Cache paragraph: 'The cache read-check-write is protected by an asyncio.Lock (one lock per in-flight key, or a single verifier-level lock) so that concurrent requests for the same token coalesce into a single introspection call rather than racing. The lock is released before the result is returned to callers.' Add a corresponding AC observable oracle: 'two concurrent awaits on the same token result in exactly one introspection HTTP call.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a cache retirement/eviction policy (e.g., a maximum entry count with LRU eviction, or a periodic sweep of entries past their expiry) so the cache cannot grow without bound under token churn.
findings.finding: The plan specifies TTL-based logical expiry for cache entries but never specifies physical eviction of expired entries. Under token churn (many short-lived or single-use tokens), the cache grows without bound — a memory-leak vector. No max-size bound or LRU eviction policy is defined.
findings.location: Security section — Cache paragraph; AC bullet 3
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the Security/Cache paragraph: 'The cache is bounded to at most N entries (e.g., 1 000, operator-configurable via `auth_introspection_cache_max_size`); eviction uses LRU order. Expired entries are lazily evicted on access and eagerly evicted when the bound is reached.' Alternatively, use Python's `functools.lru_cache` wrapper or `cachetools.TTLCache` with a hard size cap.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify a concurrency guard (e.g. `asyncio.Lock` per token hash, as in `voter.py`) for the introspection cache to prevent concurrent cache-miss thundering herd and check-then-act races.
findings.finding: The plan specifies an in-process dict cache under a FastMCP async server but does not address the check-then-act race condition: two concurrent coroutines carrying the same token will simultaneously miss the cache, both fire an introspection HTTP call, and both write the result. The codebase already handles exactly this pattern via asyncio.Lock in voter.py.
findings.location: Security section — Cache subsection; also AC bullet 3 ('active:true positive is cached')
findings.suggested_fix: Add a per-token-hash asyncio.Lock (or a single coarse asyncio.Lock protecting the entire cache dict) — matching the pattern in src/rebar/review_bot/voter.py's `_lock_for` helper.

findings.finding: The cache-write placement rule is ambiguous for the case where the introspection response returns `active:true` but the subsequent *aud* validation fails. The plan states 'Only `active:true` positives are cached' but does not define whether 'positive' means (a) `active:true` alone, or (b) `active:true` AND all post-processing validations (aud, exp sanity) pass. This is a state-increment placement gap: the cache write could occur before or after aud-validation, with materially different security implications.
findings.location: Security section — 'Only `active:true` positives are cached'; also AC-4 (cache assertions)
findings.suggested_fix: Add to Security: 'A result is cached only when `active:true` AND all local validations (aud, exp sanity) pass. An active:true response that fails aud-validation is treated as a non-acceptance and is never cached.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add S1 to the declared dependencies in the Scope section (e.g. 'Depends on S1 and S2') to match the What section and first AC's explicit reliance on S1's generator fixes.
findings.finding: The Scope/dependencies section declares 'Depends on S2. Independent of S3/S5/S6.' but both the What section and the first AC explicitly depend on S1's generator fixes ('via S1's generator fixes that make them iterate MCP_ENV_VARS'). This is a cross-section contradiction: the declared dependency list omits S1, yet the docs AC is only passable if S1 has already landed.
findings.location: Scope section ('Depends on S2. Independent of S3/S5/S6.') vs. What section and AC line 1 ('via S1's fixes making gen_mcp_reference.py/gen_env_registry.py iterate MCP_ENV_VARS')
findings.suggested_fix: Change 'Depends on S2.' to 'Depends on S1 and S2.' in the Scope section.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm and name the specific FastMCP API call that yields an ASGI-compatible app for `httpx.ASGITransport` injection (e.g., verify `FastMCP.asgi_app()` or `FastMCP.http_app()` exists in mcp>=1.9 and name it in the integration-test AC), or note a short spike to discover the correct test-transport mounting pattern.
findings.finding: The integration test AC specifies 'httpx ASGITransport wrapping the MCP server', but the rebar MCP server is a FastMCP stdio server (build_server().run() is stdio transport). It is Partially-verified that FastMCP from mcp>=1.9 exposes an ASGI application interface suitable for httpx ASGITransport — the plan does not name the specific FastMCP attribute/method that yields the ASGI app (e.g., `mcp.asgi_app()` or similar), leaving a potential capability gap if FastMCP's ASGI surface differs from what the plan assumes.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — integration test bullet ('httpx ASGITransport wrapping the MCP server')

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit requirement in the Security section that TLS certificate validation MUST be enforced (default httpx behavior; no verify=False / no custom SSL context that disables chain validation unless an explicit CA bundle path is configured), and add an AC oracle that the httpx client used for introspection does NOT disable certificate verification.
findings.finding: The plan declares 'HTTPS-only' (scheme-check at construction) but never specifies that TLS certificate validation must be enforced. An implementor supporting internal CAs or self-signed AS certificates could set httpx verify=False or supply a permissive SSL context without violating the plan's spec, silently defeating the TLS boundary protection against a MITM on the introspection channel.
findings.location: Security section — 'Call the introspection endpoint over authenticated TLS … HTTPS-only (an http:// endpoint is rejected at construction)'
findings.suggested_fix: Extend the Security section with: 'TLS certificate validation MUST be enforced (httpx default). Disabling certificate verification (verify=False or equivalent) is not permitted; operators needing a private CA supply an explicit CA-bundle path, not disabled validation.' Add to the construction-time-guards AC: 'and that the httpx client used for introspection does not set verify=False.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Explicitly specify `httpx.AsyncClient` (not `httpx.Client`) for the introspection HTTP call, matching the codebase pattern in `src/rebar/llm/runner.py` and preserving the async event loop.
findings.finding: The plan says 'Reuse the already-present httpx' for the introspection HTTP call but does not specify whether to use `httpx.AsyncClient` (async) or `httpx.Client` (sync). FastMCP executes verifier logic inside async coroutines; using `httpx.Client` (sync) would block the event loop for every introspection round-trip.
findings.location: Approach & rationale section; Security section — 'bounded timeout'
findings.suggested_fix: Add one sentence to the Approach section: 'The verifier uses `httpx.AsyncClient` (not the sync `httpx.Client`) so the event loop is not blocked during introspection round-trips.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC assertion (or extend an existing unit-test AC) that verifies the `client_id` fallback: when the introspection response includes `client_id` it maps directly; when absent but `sub` is present it falls back to `sub`; when both are absent it is `"unknown"`.
findings.finding: The What section describes a `client_id` fallback mapping ('client_id→client_id (absent → falls back to sub, else "unknown")') as a defined deliverable of the AccessToken construction, but no AC verifies this fallback behavior. This is an uncovered deliverable.
findings.location: What section (AccessToken mapping paragraph) vs. all AC lines
findings.suggested_fix: Extend the first unit-test AC (active:true positive path) with: 'and when the response omits client_id but includes sub, AccessToken.client_id==sub; when both are absent, client_id=="unknown"'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an observability requirement to the Security or Approach section: introspection failures (non-200, timeout, malformed response) MUST emit a structured log entry at WARN/ERROR level (excluding token/secret values) with the endpoint URL and error class; add a corresponding AC.
findings.finding: The plan introduces a new external failure point (the RFC 7662 introspection endpoint) but specifies no observability — no structured log on failure, no metric, no alert hook. Operators cannot detect a misconfigured endpoint, a downed AS, or a systematic rejection pattern without grepping application crashes.
findings.location: Security section — 'Fail-closed transport'; Scope section
findings.suggested_fix: Add to Security: 'On any introspection error (non-200, timeout, connection error, malformed JSON), the verifier MUST emit a structured log entry at WARNING level containing: the endpoint URL, the HTTP status or exception class, and a request-id if available — never the token or secret. The log entry MUST be asserted in a unit test (observable oracle: a mock logger captures at least one WARNING call on error paths).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify that `httpx` is declared as an explicit direct dependency in `pyproject.toml` under `[project.optional-dependencies].mcp` (or core) rather than relying on it as a transitive of `mcp>=1.9`, and update the plan's Approach section to note this requirement.
findings.finding: The plan asserts 'Reuse the already-present httpx' but httpx is not declared as a direct dependency in pyproject.toml — it is only present transitively (via mcp>=1.9 or pydantic-ai-slim[anthropic,retries] under [agents]). The new IntrospectionTokenVerifier lives in the mcp extra, where httpx is a transitive of mcp>=1.9, but transitive deps are not guaranteed stable across version bumps. No explicit httpx pin or direct declaration exists in [project.optional-dependencies].mcp.
findings.location: Approach & Rationale section — 'Reuse the already-present httpx'
findings.suggested_fix: Add `"httpx>=0.27"` to `[project.optional-dependencies].mcp` in pyproject.toml alongside `mcp>=1.9`, and update the Approach section to say 'add httpx as an explicit [mcp]-extra dependency' rather than 'reuse the already-present httpx'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `httpx` as an explicit direct dependency in the `[mcp]` extra in `pyproject.toml` (e.g., `mcp = ["mcp>=1.9", "httpx>=0.27"]`) so the introspection verifier's httpx imports are covered by a declared, resolver-visible dep rather than relying on a transitive.
findings.finding: The plan asserts 'Reuse the already-present httpx' but `httpx` is not declared as a direct dependency in pyproject.toml — it is only available transitively via `mcp>=1.9` in the `[mcp]` extra. The introspection verifier will directly import and use httpx (AsyncClient, BasicAuth, etc.), making it a direct dependency; relying on a transitive means a future mcp SDK update that drops or pins-away httpx would silently break the verifier in production without a resolver error.
findings.location: Approach & rationale section ('Reuse the already-present httpx') / pyproject.toml [project.optional-dependencies] mcp entry
findings.suggested_fix: In pyproject.toml, change `mcp = ["mcp>=1.9"]` to `mcp = ["mcp>=1.9", "httpx>=0.27"]` (or whatever floor the verifier's AsyncClient/BasicAuth API requires).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a concrete proving command for the markdown doc check in AC1, e.g. `python scripts/gen_mcp_reference.py && grep -c 'auth_introspection_endpoint' docs/mcp-reference.md docs/env-vars.md` (expecting non-zero counts in both files).
findings.finding: AC1 asserts the new config keys 'appear in the regenerated docs/mcp-reference.md/docs/env-vars.md' but provides no concrete proving command to verify the markdown file contents, only `scripts/check_server_manifest.py` (which checks the JSON manifest). An agent can satisfy the script check while leaving the markdown docs unverified.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC1
findings.suggested_fix: Append to AC1: 'Proving command: `python scripts/gen_mcp_reference.py && python scripts/gen_env_registry.py && grep -c auth_introspection_endpoint docs/mcp-reference.md docs/env-vars.md` — both counts must be ≥ 1.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a requirement to capture the custom-verifier rationale (why the SDK example is not imported, the security-model choices) in CLAUDE.md or an ADR, and reference it from a module-level docstring on IntrospectionTokenVerifier.
findings.finding: The architectural decision to write a custom IntrospectionTokenVerifier instead of importing the SDK's example class — and the security model choices (cache-key design, fail-closed on missing aud, private-host guard) — are not captured in any persistent artifact (ADR, CLAUDE.md, or design doc). Future maintainers encountering the class have no documented rationale for why the SDK example was not used.
findings.location: What section — 'NOTE: the SDK's IntrospectionTokenVerifier is an example, not an exported/hardened class — this story WRITES its own'; Approach section
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the plan's Compatibility or Approach section: 'A brief entry in CLAUDE.md (or docs/adr/NNNN-introspection-verifier.md) records: (a) why the SDK IntrospectionTokenVerifier example is not imported, (b) the cache-key security rationale, and (c) the fail-closed aud/private-host defaults.' Add an AC: 'CLAUDE.md or an ADR file is updated with the rationale.'

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
